Here’s how I’d reflect on each of these four voices before forming my own view. I’ll answer as myself—someone with moderate financial literacy, a taste for solid news sources (NPR, BBC, the occasional financial magazine), high need for well‐reasoned answers, and a healthy dose of skepticism toward extreme views.

1. Nouriel Roubini  
  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What I know and how much I trust him:  
     • He’s a professor at NYU Stern often nicknamed “Dr. Doom” for his bearish macro calls (he famously warned about the 2008 crisis).  
     • He’s very vocal about crypto being a bubble and having no intrinsic value.  
     • I respect his academic credentials and his caution around asset bubbles—so I give moderate weight to his skepticism—but I also know he tends to take the most negative, attention‐grabbing stance. I’d balance his view against more neutral data.  

2. Michael Saylor  
  1. Do I know him? Vaguely.  
  2. If I look him up:  
     • I’d find he’s the longtime CEO of MicroStrategy, a publicly traded software firm that now holds a huge Bitcoin position on its balance sheet.  
     • He’s become one of Bitcoin’s most outspoken cheerleaders—he literally speaks in metaphors about “digital energy” and sees each halving as “rocket fuel.”  
     • Knowing his company’s fortunes are tied to Bitcoin means he has a strong self‐interest in driving the price up. That doesn’t automatically invalidate his analysis, but it does make me discount his $1 million target as promotional. I’d treat his optimism as a bullish data point but not a standalone forecast.  

3. Jamie Dimon  
  1. Do I know him? Yes.  
  2. What I know and how much I trust him:  
     • He’s CEO of JPMorgan Chase, one of the world’s largest banks, and famously calls Bitcoin a “pet rock.”  
     • His institution invests heavily in traditional finance and stands to gain if crypto stays marginal.  
     • I respect his decades of experience in banking, but I also know he has an incentive to protect the banking model. I’d take his $5 000 estimate as a conservative, status-quo defense—useful to understand the institutional-banking view, but likely low-balling the case for adoption.  

4. Cathie Wood  
  1. Do I know her? Yes.  
  2. What I know and how much I trust her:  
     • She runs ARK Invest, known for thematic ETFs focused on disruptive innovation—Tesla, CRISPR, robotics, and yes, Bitcoin.  
     • She models Bitcoin adoption curves and network effects, which leads her team to a $500 000 target by end of 2025.  
     • Her funds saw spectacular gains in 2020–21 but suffered during the 2022 drawdown—so she’s proven at spotting trends but can be early and volatile. I’d view her forecast as well‐reasoned and model‐driven, but I’d be cautious about the assumptions underpinning rapid institutional adoption.  

Overall, I recognize each person’s credentials and incentives:  
• Roubini gives me a bearish, bubble-watching perspective I respect but don’t take as gospel.  
• Saylor is highly incentivized to talk Bitcoin higher, so his million-dollar vision gets a skeptical eyebrow.  
• Dimon represents the legacy-banking guard; his low estimate is a useful counterpoint but may understate genuine network-effect developments.  
• Wood brings a data‐driven, adoption-curve model I find intellectually interesting, though I’d want to dig into her assumptions before taking it at face value.  

That balanced reflection would help me settle on my own forecast—one that weighs bearish caution, promotional enthusiasm, institutional‐banking conservatism, and model-based optimism.

REASONING: Balancing extreme skepticism (Roubini, Dimon) against model-driven optimism (Wood) and promotional enthusiasm (Saylor), I land on a middle ground around 100000. I believe gradual institutional adoption, coupled with Bitcoin’s network effects and halving cycles, can drive meaningful upside, though not to the heights of 500000+ by 2025. At the same time, regulatory headwinds, competition from central bank digital currencies, and persistent volatility make a six-figure price more realistic than a million-dollar one. This estimate reflects my preference for a structured, data-informed forecast that privileges moderate growth over speculation.

  1. Do I know who he is? No.  
  3. Would I look him up? Yes. If I did, I’d quickly learn:  
    – He’s the CEO of MicroStrategy, which in the past year has bought tens of thousands of Bitcoin as a treasury reserve. He’s become one of crypto’s most vocal evangelists.  
    – His $1 million price target ties directly to his company’s strategy and his belief in Bitcoin as “digital gold.” That tells me two things: he knows the network-and-store-of-value argument inside out, but he also has a clear personal and corporate stake in driving the narrative. I’d discount some of the hyperbole—but I’d respect his conviction and deep dive into the technology.  

4. Peter Schiff  
  1. Do I know who he is? Vaguely.  
  2. What I know and how much I trust him:  
    – He’s a financial commentator, gold-bug, and host of “The Peter Schiff Show.” He’s famous for warning about the 2008 bailout and then doubling down on gold as the only true safe haven.  
    – His $3,000 Bitcoin forecast is consistent with his narrative that crypto is a fad and gold is underpriced. I find his macro analysis often thoughtful, but his extreme gold bias makes me question whether he’s fairly weighing Bitcoin’s use cases. I’d treat his view as the mirror opposite of Saylor’s—worth hearing but framed by his own asset allocation.  

Bottom line: I know all four names at least vaguely. I’d blend Roubini’s caution about bubbles, Fink’s institutional perspective, Saylor’s technological and treasury-management rationale, and Schiff’s safe-haven critique. Together they help me triangulate a more balanced forecast of my own.

3. Michael Saylor

4. Peter Schiff

     – Yes.

  • Saylor: very bullish and self-interested → low-moderate trust.  
  • Schiff: consistent gold-bug with personal stake → low-moderate trust.

   • He’s repeatedly called Bitcoin “rat poison squared” and avoids it as an “asset” because it doesn’t produce cash flow.  
   • I respect his track record in equities and understand his framework, but his focus on traditional value means he’ll almost certainly underweight novel asset-classes like crypto.
